samples.xml_description:
Implement an asynchronous FIFO, FIFO bit width and depth can be configured(parameter DEPTH = 16, parameter WIDTH = 8). The asynchronous FIFO structure is divided into several parts. The first part is dual-port RAM, which is used for data storage. Instantiate dual-port RAM as a submodule, The RAM ports are input wclk, input wenc, input [$clog2(DEPTH)-1:0] waddr, input [WIDTH-1:0] wdata, input rclk, input renc, input [$clog2(DEPTH)-1:0] raddr, output reg [WIDTH-1:0] rdata. The second part is the data write controller. The third part is the data read controller. The fourth part is the read pointer synchronizer. The read pointer is collected using the two-stage trigger of the write clock and output to the data write controller. The fifth part is the write pointer synchronizer, which uses the two-stage trigger of the read clock to collect the write pointer and output it to the data read controller.

The method of empty and full judgment is to generate empty and full signals by comparing the Gray code. Use 4-bit Gray code as a read/write pointer for a FIFO with depth 8. The gray code is converted to a four-digit binary number, using the lower three digits of the binary number as the address to access RAM. When the read and write Pointers are equal, the FIFO is null.

When the write pointer has one more cycle RAM than the read pointer, the highest and second-highest bits of the read and write pointer are opposite, the remaining bits are the same, and the FIFO is full. Write clock signal used for synchronous write operations. Read clock signal used for synchronous read operations. Active-low reset signal for both read and write operations. Write increment signal. Triggered when a new data is written into the FIFO. Read increment signal. Triggered when data is read from the FIFO. Write data input. Data to be written into the FIFO. Write full signal. Indicates if the FIFO is full and cannot accept further write operations. Read empty signal. Indicates if the FIFO is empty and cannot provide any data for read operations. Read data output. Data read from the FIFO. Parameter to configure the bit-width of the data input and output. Default value is 8. Parameter to configure the depth of the FIFO. Default value is 16.

1. Instantiate dual-port RAM as a submodule:

<module id="dual_port_RAM"> <description>Implement a dual-port RAM module with separate read and write ports.&description> <parameter_description> <param id="DEPTH">Depth of the RAM.¶m> <param id="WIDTH">Width of the RAM.¶m> </parameter_description> <input id="wclk">Write clock.&input> <input id="wenc">Write enable. Active high.&input> <input id="waddr" width_description="$clog2(DEPTH)">Write address.&input> <input id="wdata" width_description="WIDTH-1:0">Write data.&input> <input id="rclk">Read clock.&input> <input id="renc">Read enable. Active high.&input> <input id="raddr" width_description="$clog2(DEPTH)">Read address.&input> <output id="rdata" width_description="WIDTH-1:0">Read data.&output> </module>

2. Connect input and output signals to the dual-port RAM module:

<implementation> <p>Connect the input and output signals to the dual-port RAM module.&p> <code> <wdata> = wdata; <rdata> = data_out; <wen> = winc; <ren> = rinc; </code> </implementation>

3. Implement logic for managing write and read pointers:

<implementation> <p>1. Write pointer logic.&p> <code> always @(posedge wclk or negedge rstn) begin if (~rstn) begin waddr_bin <= 0; end else if (winc && ~wfull) begin waddr_bin <= waddr_bin + 1; end end </code> <p>2. Read pointer logic.&p> <code> always @(posedge rclk or negedge rstn) begin if (~rstn) begin raddr_bin <= 0; end else if (rinc && ~rempty) begin raddr_bin <= raddr_bin + 1; end end </code> </implementation>

4. Implement Gray code conversion for write and read pointers:

<implementation> <p>1. Write pointer Gray code logic.&p> <code> always @(posedge wclk or negedge rstn) begin if (~rstn) begin wptr <= 0; end else if (winc && ~wfull) begin wptr <= wptr ^ (wptr >> 1); end end </code> <p>2. Read pointer Gray code logic.&p> <code> always @(posedge rclk or negedge rstn) begin if (~rstn) begin rptr <= 0; end else if (rinc && ~rempty) begin rptr <= rptr ^ (rptr >> 1); end end </code> </implementation>

5. Implement pointer buffer logic:

<implementation> <p>1. Write pointer buffer logic.&p> <code> always @(posedge wclk or negedge rstn) begin if (~rstn) begin wptr_buff <= 0; end else begin wptr_buff <= wptr; end end </code> <p>2. Read pointer buffer logic.&p> <code> always @(posedge rclk or negedge rstn) begin if (~rstn) begin rptr_buff <= 0; end else begin rptr_buff <= rptr; end end </code> </implementation>

6. Implement full and empty signal logic:

<implementation> <p>1. Full signal logic.&p> <code> always @(posedge wclk or negedge rstn) begin if (~rstn) begin wfull <= 0; end else if (wptr == ~rptr_buff && wptr[$clog2(DEPTH)] != rptr_buff[$clog2(DEPTH)]) begin wfull <= 1; end else begin wfull <= 0; end end </code> <p>2. Empty signal logic.&p> <code> always @(posedge rclk or negedge rstn) begin if (~rstn) begin rempty <= 0; end else if (rptr == wptr_buff && rptr[$clog2(DEPTH)] != wptr_buff[$clog2(DEPTH)]) begin rempty <= 1;
